summary: American photographer (1890–1976) and cinematographer, known for pioneering 'straight photography' as a shift from Pictorialism. Best known for Wall Street (1915), showing silhouetted figures dwarfed by the dark rectangular windows of the J.P. Morgan building. Also known for White Fence, Blind (a woman wearing a 'Blind' sign, taken with a false/prism lens), and the documentary film Manhatta (co-directed with Charles Sheeler). Student of Lewis Hine; his work was shown at Alfred Stieglitz's 291 Gallery and published in Camera Work. Inspired Ansel Adams and Georgia O'Keeffe.

works.name: Wall Street (1915)
works.description: Paul Strand's most famous photograph. Shows silhouetted men walking below four (or five) huge, dark, semi-abstract rectangular windows of the J.P. Morgan building. Long shadows stretch out beneath the figures. Published in the 49th and 50th issues of Alfred Stieglitz's Camera Work. Considered a pivotal work in the shift from Pictorialism to Modernist 'straight photography.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows silhouetted pedestrians dwarfed by the huge dark rectangular windows of the J.P. Morgan building; long shadows; considered a landmark of 'straight photography'
examples: This photographer of The Destroyed Room portrayed a non-accidental accident by having paper thrown into an airstream in his A Sudden Gust of Wind, which recreates a Hokusai work.
A work of 'straight photography' taken in this city depicts a series of people with long shadows walking beneath five large, black, semi-abstract windows of a bank.

works.name: Blind (1916)
works.description: Candid portrait of an old woman with one eye shut and the other staring blankly, wearing a cardboard sign reading 'Blind' around her neck. Taken with a false lens (prism lens) that pointed 90 degrees away from the subject so she would not realize she was being photographed. The approach of using a camera that 'shot ninety degrees off from the opposite direction' was used for multiple candid street portraits. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows a woman wearing a sign reading 'Blind' around her neck; taken with a false/prism lens pointing 90 degrees away from the subject
examples: This man used a special camera that shot ninety degrees off from the opposite direction in which it was pointed to take candid photographs such as one of a beggar holding a sign saying 'Blind.'

Both Yawning Woman and Sandwich Man were likely shot, as was a photo of a woman with one closed eye and another staring off to the side while wearing a sign that reads 'blind', using this photographer's false lens.

works.name: White Fence (1916)
works.description: Photograph showing an extremely flat white fence that forms a diagonal line in the foreground, with two Port Kent, New York houses and a barn partially blurred behind it. One of Strand's examples of abstraction and straight photography. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Shows a white fence forming a diagonal line in the foreground below two Port Kent, New York houses; extremely flat composition
examples: The title object forms a diagonal line below two Port Kent, New York houses in this man's extremely-flat White Fence.
His other photos include The White Fence and one of an old woman wearing a sign reading 'Blind.'

works.name: Manhatta (1921)
works.description: Short avant-garde city-symphony documentary film co-directed and co-photographed by Strand and Charles Sheeler. Intertitles from Walt Whitman poems are interspersed throughout. The film borrowed from Strand's Wall Street photograph. Considered a pioneer of the 'city-symphony' genre. Wikipedia

clues.clue: City-symphony documentary film co-directed with Charles Sheeler; intertitles from Walt Whitman poems throughout; pioneered the city-symphony genre
examples: Name this photographer who shot half of the documentary Manhatta.
This student of Lewis Hine collaborated on a film with Charles Sheeler which borrowed from his most famous photo of silhouetted men walking in front of a building.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: American photographer (1890–1976). Student of Lewis Hine; his interest in photography began when Hine took him to Alfred Stieglitz's 291 Gallery. Work published in Camera Work (the 49th and 50th issues featured his pictures). Worked in Mexico, where he was head of the Education Department's Photo and Film division; work there includes Woman of Alvarado, Veracruz. Photographed an old widow surrounded by her five sons in Italy (trip taken with the screenwriter of Bicycle Thieves). Photographed a Church at the Taos art colony in New Mexico. Inspired Ansel Adams to move away from Pictorialism. His close-cropping technique inspired Georgia O'Keeffe's large-scale flower paintings. Strand's work signals a shift from Pictorialism to 'straight photography.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Student of Lewis Hine; interest in photography began when Hine took him to Stieglitz's 291 Gallery
examples: Strand's interest in photography began when Lewis Hine took him to visit this gallery [291].
That photo was once credited to Lewis Hine, who taught this photographer of Wall Street.

clues.clue: Inspired Ansel Adams to move away from Pictorialism
examples: This artist was inspired by Paul Strand to move away from the Pictorialist style.
This artist created an image of a rose petal on a piece of driftwood after an inspirational meeting with Paul Strand.

clues.clue: His close-cropping technique inspired Georgia O'Keeffe's large-scale flower paintings
examples: An artist who used photographic techniques taught by Paul Strand to paint these objects [flowers] asked Mabel Dodge Luhan to write about them from a feminist perspective.
Georgia O'Keeffe painted many cow skulls and, inspired by Paul Strand's close-cropping techniques, these objects [flowers].

clues.clue: Worked in Mexico; became head of the Education Department's Photo and Film division there
examples: A hooded woman looks to the right in this artist's Woman of Alvarado, Veracruz, one of his acclaimed Mexico works that got him a job as the head of the Education Department's Photo and Film division.

clues.clue: Photographed an old widow surrounded by her five sons in Italy, influenced by neorealism; trip taken with the screenwriter of Bicycle Thieves
examples: Strand photographed an old widow surrounded by her five sons in this country [Italy]. This country's genre of neorealism influenced that photo, which Strand took on a trip with the screenwriter of its film Bicycle Thieves.

clues.clue: Work published in Camera Work (issues 49 and 50); shown at Stieglitz's 291 Gallery
examples: A picture of a bearded man holding a placard reading 'The Price' by this artist appeared in the 49th and 50th issues of Camera Work.
This photographer and principal member of the Photo-Secessionist movement displayed some of the earliest work of Paul Strand at the 291 Gallery.

comprehensive_summary:
Paul Strand (1890–1976) was an American photographer and cinematographer who was a pivotal figure in the shift from Pictorialism to modernist 'straight photography.' He was a student of Lewis Hine, and his interest in photography began when Hine took him to Alfred Stieglitz's 291 Gallery. His work was published in issues 49 and 50 of Stieglitz's Camera Work.

His most famous photograph, Wall Street (1915), depicts silhouetted pedestrians dwarfed by the huge dark rectangular windows of the J.P. Morgan building, with long shadows stretching beneath them. Blind (1916) shows an old woman with one eye closed and the other staring blankly, wearing a 'Blind' sign around her neck; it was taken with a false (prism) lens pointing 90 degrees away from the subject so she would not know she was being photographed. White Fence (1916) is an extremely flat composition showing a white fence forming a diagonal line in the foreground before two Port Kent, New York houses.

In 1921, Strand co-directed and co-photographed Manhatta with Charles Sheeler, a short city-symphony documentary with intertitles from Walt Whitman poems, considered a pioneer of the city-symphony genre. He also worked in Mexico, becoming head of the Education Department's Photo and Film division, and created Woman of Alvarado, Veracruz. He photographed a church at the Taos art colony in New Mexico. He traveled to Italy (with the screenwriter of Bicycle Thieves) and photographed an old widow surrounded by her five sons.

Strand's work had notable influence on other artists: he inspired Ansel Adams to move away from Pictorialism, and his close-cropping photographic technique inspired Georgia O'Keeffe's large-scale flower paintings.
recursive_suggestions: Alfred Stieglitz — Strand's mentor and gallery owner; published Strand's work in Camera Work
Ansel Adams — repeatedly mentioned as inspired by Strand to abandon Pictorialism
Georgia O'Keeffe — repeatedly mentioned as inspired by Strand's close-cropping technique
Photo-Secessionism — Strand's work signals the movement's turn to modernism; could have its own topic

Photograph: extremely flat composition showing a white fence forming a diagonal line in the foreground before two Port Kent, New York houses

Artist: student of Lewis Hine; interest in photography began when Hine took him to Alfred Stieglitz's 291 Gallery
Artist: his meeting with Ansel Adams inspired Adams to abandon Pictorialism; his close-cropping technique inspired Georgia O'Keeffe's large-scale flower paintings
Artist: worked in Mexico, becoming head of the Education Department's Photo and Film division; photographed Woman of Alvarado, Veracruz
Artist: traveled to Italy with the screenwriter of Bicycle Thieves and photographed an old widow surrounded by her five sons, influenced by neorealism
